title: A Call for Psycho-Affective Change: Fanon, Feminism, and White Negrophobic Femininity
author: Yokum

opening_move.description: Opens by diagnosing that Susan Brownmiller's virulent critique of Fanon in Against Our Will — charging him with misogyny, personal anguish, and confusion — has remained unanswered and standing, despite being widely cited. The puzzle: is Brownmiller's charge that Fanon hates women and obsesses over rape justified, or does it misread Fanon's sociogenic and psychoanalytic project? The paper defends Fanon against this charge.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Fanon's analysis of white negrophobic femininity is best interpreted not as personal misogyny or justification of rape but as an acerbic critique of colonial society that produces such psycho-affective disorders — ultimately calling for psycho-affective change and liberation for both colonized Blacks and colonizing whites.

rivals_engaged: Brownmiller's reading of Fanon as misogynist
feminist critics who see Fanon as antifeminist (hooks, Bergner, Kalisa)
decontextualized readings of Fanon's rape fantasy passages
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of Brownmiller: argues her charge misreads Fanon by ignoring (1) his sociogenic project (not merely psychoanalytic — the colonial situation is the pathological agent); (2) the critical-theoretical function of his discussion of negrophobia (diagnose to destroy); (3) the distinction between reporting/analyzing rape fantasies and condoning rape. The reconstruction of Fanon shows his account of white negrophobic femininity as a critique of colonial society, not an attack on white women.

data_sources: Fanon's The Wretched of the Earth
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Philosophical hermeneutics: careful contextual reading of disputed passages in Fanon; reconstruction of his psychoanalytic and sociogenic method; immanent critique of Brownmiller through close reading; comparison with how Sharpley-Whiting and Rabaka read the same material.
epistemic_mode.notes: This is primarily a hermeneutic-rehabilitative paper: its primary goal is to correctly read Fanon, not to derive a normative political conclusion for the present. The 'call for psycho-affective change' in the title is Fanon's normative claim, not Yokum's independent normative thesis. Classified purely_interpretive_hermeneutic. Compare to engelman_2023 (Feuerbach-Marx senses) and kastrinakis_2024 (Adorno-Nietzsche): all three are interpretive rehabilitations without independent normative conclusion. Distinct from dyson_2024 (which reconstructs Fanon to derive an independent normative argument about institutional psychotherapy).

normative_stake.domain: Fanon studies / feminist philosophy / postcolonial theory / race

confidence_notes: The classification purely_interpretive_hermeneutic rests on the paper's primary function: defending a correct reading of Fanon's Black Skin, White Masks against misreading. The paper does not derive an independent normative claim about what should be done in the present; the 'call for psycho-affective change' is Fanon's own conclusion, which Yokum reconstructs and defends. High confidence.
